coredata.dc:title: Non-enzymatic hydrogen peroxide sensor based on graphene quantum dots-chitosan/methylene blue hybrid nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Mollarasouli, Fariba
Asadpour-Zeynali, Karim
Campuzano, Susana
Yáñez-Sedeño, Paloma
Pingarrón, José M.
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) functionalized with chitosan (GQDs-CS) were used for the first time as a suitable nanostructured sensing film for efficient immobilization of methylene blue (MB) through amino-hydroxyl reaction to prepare a novel non-enzymatic hydrogen peroxide sensor using a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The synthesized hybrid nanostructures were characterized by X-ray diffraction, field emission scanning electron microscopy, cyclic voltammetry, FT-IR, UV–vis, photoluminescence, and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy techniques. Cyclic voltammograms showed that the GQDs-CS/MB/GCE exhibited a significant electrocatalytic activity for the reduction of H2O2. The calculated kcat is 4.45×104 cm3mol−1s−1. The calibration graph for H2O2 constructed by amperometry (−0.6V vs. SCE) at the modified electrode showed two different linear ranges (1.0×10−6–2.9×10−3 M and 2.9–11.78mM) with a sensitivity of 10.115μA/mM for the lower linear range and a calculated detection limit of 0.7μM (S/N=3). The response time of the sensor for H2O2 detection was 3s. The electrochemical response of GQDs-CS/MB/GCE is not influenced by potential interferents (ascorbic and uric acids, dopamine, caffeine, glucose, and various inorganic salts). This modified electrode exhibited suitability for the non-enzymatic H2O2 sensing in food and water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Methylene blue (MB)
Chitosan (CS)
Non-enzymatic electrochemical sensor
H2O2

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots-chitosan (GQDs-CS) nanocomposites
Determination in honey, fruit juice and water samples
Characterization and surface morphology of the nanocomposites
Optimization the conditions of MB electrodeposition on GQDs-CS/GCE
Electrocatalytic activity of GQDs-CS/MB/GC electrode for reduction of H2O2
Effect of pH and the type of supporting electrolyte
Effect of the potential scan rate
Chronoamperometric responses and estimation of the diffusion coefficient of H2O2
Electrochemical detection of H2O2 at the GQDs-CS/MB/GCE
Reproducibility of the measurements and stability of GQDs-CS/MB/GCE

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Redox reaction mechanism of MB electrodeposited on the GQDs-CS/GC electrode surface.

FESEM images of the bare GCE (A), GQDs/GCE (B), GQDs-CS/GCE with different magnification (C, D) and GQDs-CS/MB/GCE with different magnification (E, F), (G) and (H) Energy dispersive X-ray spectra (EDX) of GQDs-CS/GCE and GQDs-CS/MB/GCE, respectively. TEM images of GQDs (I-K) and GQDs-CS (L).
UV–vis (A), photoluminescence (B) and FT-IR (C) spectra of GQDs (a), GQDs-CS (b) and GQDs-CS/MB nanocomposites (c), and XRD pattern of GQDs nanocomposites (D).

Optimization of electrodeposition potential (A) electrodeposition time (B) and MB concentration (C) used for immobilization of MB onto GQDs-CS/GCE. Error bars were estimated as triple of the standard deviation.

Cyclic voltammograms of different modified electrodes in 0.1M KCl containing 1mMK3 [Fe (CN)6] (A) and in 0.1M phosphate buffer containing 0.1M Na2SO4 (pH 7.4) in the absence (a, b, c, d) and presence (e) of 0.1mM H2O2 (B). Bare GCE (a), GQDs/GCE (b), GQDs-CS/GCE (c) and GQDs-CS/MB/GCE (d,e). Cyclic voltammograms of GQDs-CS/MB/GCE in Britton-Robinson buffer at different pH values (from right to left), 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7; 7.4, 8, 9, 10, 11 and 12. Inset: (a) and (b) are the variation of formal potentials and the anodic peak current with pH values, respectively; scan rate of CVs=50 mVs−1.

Cyclic voltammograms recorded at GQDs-CS/MB/GCE in 0.1M phosphate buffer containing 0.1M Na2SO4 (pH 7.4) at different scan rates (from inner to outer: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 80, 90, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200 and 250mVs−1). Plot of peak currents vs. the scan rate (A). Variation of peak potential vs. log ν (B) and log current vs. potential (C).

Chronoamperograms obtained at the GQDs-CS/MB/GCE in the absence (a) and in the presence (b–i) of 100, 297, 395, 496, 594, 690, 785 and 877μM H2O2 at −0.6V vs SCE. Inset A: plots (b–i) of I versus t−1/2 for various H2O2 concentrations (100–877μM); inset B: Slope values of I-t−1/2 plots (c-i) versus H2O2 concentrations; inset C: dependence of icat/id on t1/2 derived from chronoamperograms corresponding to curves a and h; Supporting electrolyte: 0.1M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.1M Na2SO4.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms obtained at GQDs-CS/MB/GCE for increasing concentrations of H2O2 (from inner to outer: 0, 5, 15, 50, 100, 300, 400, 500, 600 and 800μM H2O2); scan rate: 50mVs−1. Inset: calibration graph obtained by CV for H2O2. (B) Typical steady-state response of GQDs-CS/MB/GCE to successive additions of H2O2 to the stirring supporting electrolyte. Inset (a) the magnification of the red-circled part from a-p: 1, 2, 4.97, 14.77, 29.12, 47.62, 71.1, 94.34, 117.3, 140.18, 162.8, 196.3, 240.2, 283.4, 327.3, and 370.7μM (b) plot of the cathodic current vs. H2O2 concentration. Supporting electrolyte and applied potential as in Fig. 6.

Amperometric responses of the GQDs-CS/MB/GCE for successive additions of 0.5mM H2O2 (a and b) and 1mM of (c) glucose, (d) ascorbic acid, (e) uric acid, (f) dopamine, (g) caffeine, (h) KNO3, (i) Mg(NO3)2, (j) Pb(NO3)2, (k) AgNO3, (l) Cd(NO3)2, (m) NaNO2, (n) NaF, (o) Na2SO4 and 0.5mM H2O2 (p and q). Supporting electrolyte and applied potential as in Fig. 6.
Comparison of the analytical characteristics of some proposed methods for the electrochemical determination of H2O2 using different (bio)sensors.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: LOD: detection limit; LDR: linear dynamic range.
RB-MWCNT: Reactive blue 19- Multiwalled carbon nanotube.
Hb: Hemoglobin; LDH-SDS: Layered double hydroxide-Sodium dodecylsulfonate.
GS@CeO2/Au: gold nanoparticles loading on graphene sheets@cerium oxide.
CB: Celestine blue; HRP: Horseradish peroxidase; OANP: Oleylamine nanoparticle (gold); OANW: Oleylamine nanowire (gold); N.M: Not mentioned.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of H2O2 in food and water samples (n=3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: texp: estimated value using Student t test (ttab,95% =4.3).

head.ce:title: Non-enzymatic hydrogen peroxide sensor based on graphene quantum dots-chitosan/methylene blue hybrid nanostructures

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 51666-16471, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Química Analítica, Facultad de Ciencias Químicas, Universidad Complutense de Madrid, 28040 Madrid, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: IMDEA Nanoscience, Ciudad Universitaria de Cantoblanco, 28049 Madrid, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) functionalized with chitosan (GQDs-CS) were used for the first time as a suitable nanostructured sensing film for efficient immobilization of methylene blue (MB) through amino-hydroxyl reaction to prepare a novel non-enzymatic hydrogen peroxide sensor using a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The synthesized hybrid nanostructures were characterized by X-ray diffraction, field emission scanning electron microscopy, cyclic voltammetry, FT-IR, UV–vis, photoluminescence, and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy techniques. Cyclic voltammograms showed that the GQDs-CS/MB/GCE exhibited a significant electrocatalytic activity for the reduction of H2O2. The calculated kcat is 4.45×104 cm3mol−1s−1. The calibration graph for H2O2 constructed by amperometry (−0.6V vs. SCE) at the modified electrode showed two different linear ranges (1.0×10−6–2.9×10−3 M and 2.9–11.78mM) with a sensitivity of 10.115μA/mM for the lower linear range and a calculated detection limit of 0.7μM (S/N=3). The response time of the sensor for H2O2 detection was 3s. The electrochemical response of GQDs-CS/MB/GCE is not influenced by potential interferents (ascorbic and uric acids, dopamine, caffeine, glucose, and various inorganic salts). This modified electrode exhibited suitability for the non-enzymatic H2O2 sensing in food and water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, a two-dimensional (2D) form of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms with honeycomb network structure, possesses important advantages facilitating such as heterogeneous electron transfer between electrode substrates and biomolecules [1]. Unique graphene properties such as high electrical conductivity, good biocompatibility, huge specific surface area, and mechanical flexibility have largely contributed to the great attention that this nanomaterial has attracted for sensing applications [2]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), with diameter below 20nm, are derived from both carbon dots (CDs) and graphene [3]. This 0D nanomaterial exhibits stable luminescence, high solubility in many solvents, good biocompatibility, possibility of edge functionalization and suitable conductivity which make it an excellent probe for both high contrast imaging and sensing applications [4,5]. In addition, GQDs have been proposed as a less toxic alternative to other quantum dots (CdS, CdSe, ZnS, ZnSe, PbS, HgTe, Ag2Se, Ag2S, CuInSe2, CuInS2, InAs and InP), which cannot be used in biological systems due to the presence of toxic metals. Moreover, in order to enhance their hydrophility and biocompatibility, GQDs can be easily functionalized with oxygen containing groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and epoxy groups [6].

In recent years, the use of natural polymers such as agar, carrageenan, chitosan, and chitin has attracted considerable attention as an immobilization matrix for enzyme and nano-materials. In particular, the attractive properties of chitosan (CS) such as good film forming ability, non-toxicity, chemically inert, biocompatibility, high mechanical strength, good stability, adhesion capability, and high hydrophilicity make this material a relevant immobilization matrix to be used in electrode surface modification for (bio)sensing applications [6,7]. Moreover, CS has demonstrated also to facilitate the electron transfer after its swelling in the reaction mixture due to its hydrophilic nature [6]. CS has been used recently as a dispersant to modify GQDs and prepare GQDs-CS nanocatalysts for forming stable composite films on electrode surfaces. This nanocomposite material demonstrated efficient electrocatalysis for vitamin C [6] and methanol oxidation [7].

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is an important reactive oxygen species (ROS) in living organisms generated as the products of normal aerobic metabolism and enhanced under stress conditions. H2O2 can be used as mediator in various fields such as food processing, environmental protection, pharmaceutical, chemical industry, biology, and biomedicine diagnosis [8,9]. Therefore, the sensitive detection of H2O2 is an important concern and many analytical methods involving spectroscopy [10–12], chemiluminescence [13,14], fluorimetry [15,16], chromatography [17], and electrochemistry [18–20] have been reported for such purpose. Among these, electrochemical methods are a suitable alternative because of their low cost, high sensitivity, quick response, simple instrumentation, and possibility of miniaturization. Electrochemical sensors for the determination of H2O2 can be classified in two major classes: enzymatic and non-enzymatic sensors. In the first category, direct electron transfer occurs between enzyme and electrode giving rise to mediatorless H2O2 sensors. In the second category, a mediator such as hexacyanoferrates, ferrocene derivatives, or poly (vinyl pyridine) polymer is used to shuttle the electron between H2O2 and the electrode. Limitations of enzyme electrochemical sensors such as reduced catalytic activity and sensitivity after repeated measurements, high cost, low electron transfer rate and dependence with pH and temperature, lead to the development of non-enzymatic H2O2 sensors. Methylene blue (MB), a redox dye belonging to the phenothiazines family, can be used as a mediator for these purposes: taking advantage of its electrochemical reversibility, high electron transfer rate, chemical stability and adsorption ability on carbon electrode surfaces.

In this work, a non-enzymatic hydrogen peroxide electrochemical sensor is reported using GQDs-CS/MB composites as GCE modifier. GQDs-CS were used as a suitable scaffold for the electrodeposition of MB. The electrochemical behavior and electrocatalytic properties of this new sensor toward H2O2 were characterized by cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometry. The obtained results demonstrated the great potential of GQDs-CS/MB/GCE as a sensitive and reliable amperometric sensor for direct determination H2O2 in spiked food and water samples after minimal sample treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Apparatus and electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical experiments were performed on an AUTOLAB potentiostat (model PGSTAT30) equipped with a three-electrode cell. Three–electrode system employed includes a GQDs-CS/MB modified GCE (ϕ=2.0mm purchased from Azar electrode company, Urmia, Iran) as the working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and a platinum wire electrode as the counter electrode. The pH values of solutions were adjusted with a pH/mV/ion meter (model 827 Metrohm, Utrecht, The Netherlands). Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) of GQDs-CS/MB were carried out with a Tescan Mira3 FESEM equipped with gold coating and an EDX system. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was done by a Rigaku D/max-2200X-Ray Diffractometer (Bruker AXS) using a Cu Kα source (λ=0.154056nm). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analysis was carried out using a Bruker FTIR spectrometer at room temperature (25±1°C). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu RF–540S fluorescence spectrometer. UV–visible studies were made with an Avantes diode array spectrophotometer. In order to prepare TEM samples, one drop of diluted nanoparticle solution was casted on the Cu-TEM grid coated with the amorphous carbon thin film and left to dry in air. The TEM characterization was performed using a Phillips CM 12 microscope operating at an accelerating voltage of 120kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reagents and solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Methylene blue (MB) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Chitosan (Mw =600,000-800,000, code: 428855000 with purity of ≥90) was purchased from Acros Organics. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), glucose, glacial acetic acid, K3 [Fe (CN)6] (≥99.5%) and ascorbic acid were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1M) containing Na2SO4 0.1M with adjusted pH at 7.4 was used as supporting electrolyte for determination of H2O2. All chemical reagents used in the experiment were of analytical grade and were used directly without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots-chitosan (GQDs-CS) nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.7g of glucose was weighed and dissolved in 50mL water to obtain an aqueous solution of glucose as carbon source. The solution was transferred into a 100mL Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclaved and sealed. After heating at 180°C for 4h in a muffle furnace, and further cooling at air, a yellowish brown solution was obtained. The color change from colorless to yellowish brown implies the formation of GQDs. To remove larger GQDs nanoparticles, the solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight: 2000Da). Synthesis of GQDs-CS was carried out by dissolving 0.1g of chitosan in 10mL of 1% (v/v) acetic acid and mixing with the GQDs. The mixture was heated with a conventional heater at 60°C for 30min under stirring. The as prepared GQDs-CS nanoparticles were stored in a dark place at 4°C to maintain stability for more than 1 month.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs-CS/MB modified GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Before modification, the surface of GC electrode was polished on polishing paper with 0.3μm alumina powder to mirror-like, and subsequently rinsed with deionized water. Then, the electrode was sonicated in a mixture solution of ethanol and deionized water (1:1, v:v) for 10min to remove the remaining alumina particles. 10μL of the GQDs-CS solution was casted on the GCE surface and allowed drying at room temperature to obtain a homogeneous film (GQDs-CS) on the GCE. The modified electrode needs to be electrochemically activated by applying a constant potential of +1.7mV versus SCE for 600s. Electrodeposition of MB on the activated GQDs-CS/GCE was carried out potentiostatically at −1.2V for 600s in an aqueous solution of MB 13μM prepared in 0.1molL−1 phosphate buffer and 0.1molL−1 Na2SO4 (pH 8.5). After rinsing the electrode surface with doubly distilled water, the GQDs-CS/MB/GCE is ready to be used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination in honey, fruit juice and water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practical applicability of the developed sensor, the determination of H2O2 was carried out in four different spiked samples: honey, fruit juice, spring water and tap water. The samples were stored in a dark place at room temperature prior to analysis. Honey samples were prepared by dissolving 2g of honey (accurately weighed) in 10mL of deionized water. Since this electrochemical method has the ability to determine H2O2 in these samples after minimal pretreatment, the determination was performed in all the cases by adding 1mL of the sample under study in the electrochemical cell containing 9mL supporting electrolyte (PBS 0.1M containing 0.1M Na2SO4, pH 7.4). After sample addition, eight successive additions (100μL) of a 5mM H2O2 solution were performed and the H2O2 concentrations in the samples (both endogenous and added) were determined by amperometry (−0.6V vs. SCE) in stirred solutions using the standard additions method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and Discussion
Characterization and surface morphology of the nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of GQDs, GQDs-CS, and GQDs-CS/MB nanocomposites were investigated by FESEM. Fig. 1 B shows as GQDs nanoparticles deposited on the bare GCE are approximately spherical with diameter of about 18nm uniformly distributed on the electrode surface. After casting with GQDs-CS, a wrinkled and crumpled sheet structure was clearly observed. The average diameter of nanocomposites reached 24nm, which indicated the surface of GQDs nanoparticles was functionalized and coated by CS (Fig. 1C, D). Electrodeposition of MB on the GQDs-CS/GC electrode surface (Fig. 1E, F) yielded GQDs-CS/MB nanocomposites slightly bigger than the GQDs-CS (Fig. 1C, D), but with similar morphology, uniformly distributed on the modified electrode surface, although some of them were packed together forming big spherical structures. These results suggested that the wrinkled sheet structure of GQDs-CS/MB nanocomposite provided a large specific surface area and, hence, a high surface reactivity with effective active sites which presumably lead to improve the sensitivity and stability of the resulting sensors. Moreover, EDX spectra (Fig. 1G and H) show as C, N and O, and C, N, O and S are the only elements existing in the GQDs-CS and GQDs-CS/MB modified GCEs, respectively, which confirmed that the nanocomposites were successfully synthesized with high purity.

Furthermore, to better evaluate the surface morphology of the synthesized nanostructures, characterization of GQDs and GQDs-CS were also performed by TEM technique. TEM images of GQDs (Fig. 1I–K) revealed the spherical shape nanoparticles with a smooth surface and a diameter of about 16nm distributed throughout the sample. The aggregated observed might be attributed to the self-assembly of GQDs. Fig. 1L shows the TEM image of GQDs-CS. This image is compatible with the core@shell structure of GQDs-CS in which a thin shell of chitosan polymer is formed around the dark core of GQDs embedded inside to form like a capsule. Moreover, the surface of the polymeric shell seems rather rough and wrinkled, in agreement with previously described SEM results.

Fig. 2 A shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs (a) and GQDs-CS (b) recorded between 200 and 500nm. The two distinct absorption peaks observed at ∼230 and ∼285nm corresponded to the π– π* transitions of aromatic CC bonds and the n–π* transition of CO present in the sp2 hybridization region. Fluorescence spectra of GQDs (a) and GQDs-CS (b) (Fig. 2B) were obtained in water at room temperature under excitation at 450nm. As it can be seen, both curves exhibit the maximum emission at ∼500nm. In addition, when the excitation wavelength varies from 340 to 465nm, the wavelength of the maximum peak emission was constant. This can be attributed to the uniformity of both the size and surface state of sp2 clusters in GQD, which are responsible for the GQD fluorescence.

FT-IR spectra were used to identify the functional groups of the synthesized GQDs, GQDs-CS, and GQDs-CS/MB (depicted in Fig. 2C a–c). In the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs, the absorption bands observed at 1650 and 1700cm−1 can be assigned to CC and CO stretching vibrations, respectively. The weak doublet peak at 2856 and 2924cm−1 can be attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of CH2 group in alkyl hydrocarbon. The asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of COO are observed at 1517 and 1327cm−1 followed by C-O bonding at 1022cm−1. The peaks at about 1455 and 769cm−1 are related to methylene asymmetric scissoring and CH2 rocking modes. The broad intense peak at 3395cm−1 can be attributed to the OH stretching vibrations in the carboxylic acid group. In the FT-IR spectra of GQDs-CS (b), and GQDs-CS/MB (c), the stretching of N-H bending of NH2 (amide II) was observed at 1541cm−1 and the peak of OH stretching vibration is greatly decreased. These results are in agreement with the reaction of the OH group of GQDs with CS and the involvement of the OH group of CS in bonding with MB and GQDs.

The crystal structure of GQDs was studied by X-ray diffraction. The XRD pattern of GQDs (Fig. 2D) exhibits a single peak around 2θ=21° belonging to the (002) crystal plane (JCPDS: 41-1487). Thus, GQDs have a graphitic structure and an interlayer spacing of ∼4.1Å, larger than the bulk graphite (3.34Å) distance between its (002) planes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization the conditions of MB electrodeposition on GQDs-CS/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: In order to develop a platform with the best sensitivity some key variables such as the potential, time and concentration used for MB electrodeposition on GQDs-CS/GCE were optimized.

Electrodeposition potential is one essential factor in the successful fabrication of a sensor. Therefore, different applied potentials (in the range from −0.1 to −1.3V) were studied in the electrodeposition process. As it is shown in Fig. 3 A while the best results were obtained at −1.2V (Fig. 3A), the peak current of MB (Ipa) slightly decreased when applied more negative potential values. So, a constant potential of −1.2V was selected for further studies to obtain the maximum sensitivity.

The time during which this potential was applied for the deposition of MB on GQDs-CS/GCE was also investigated. As can be seen in Fig. 3B, the current values (Ipa of MB) increased with the deposition time until 600s and then began to decline slightly. Therefore, 600s was chosen as optimum time for electrodeposition of MB.

The effect of the MB concentration on the peak current (Ipa) was also evaluated. The experimental results in Fig. 3C demonstrated that the peak current of MB increased with this concentration from 5 to 13μM and then decreased probably due to the increase in film thickness which results in a decrease in the mass transfer rate as a result of the increasing resistance. Therefore, 13μM of MB was selected as the optimum concentration for preparing the non-enzymatic hydrogen peroxide sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of GQDs-CS/MB/GC electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetry of model electroactive species (e.g., [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−]) is a very useful electrochemical tool to characterize the kinetic barrier of the interface showing an increase in the hindrance of the electron transfer process when decreasing defect density and increasing thickness of the barrier. Fig. 4 A shows cyclic voltammograms of different modified GC electrodes in a 0.1molL−1 KCl solution containing 1.0mmolL−1 of K3[Fe(CN)6]. The pair of redox peaks appearing at potential values of Epa =+405mV and Epc =−130mV at the clean but non-pretreated GCE, with a peak separation potential (ΔEp) of 535mV, showed a significant increase in the redox peak current and decrease in the peak-to-peak separation (ΔEp =120mV) after electrode modification with GQDs (Fig. 4A, curve b), indicating an enhanced electron transfer rate of ferricyanide at the modified electrode. A similar voltammetric behavior was found at the GQDs-CS/GCE (Fig. 4A, curve c). When MB was electrodeposited on the GQDs-CS electrode surface, a remarkable current increase together with the ΔEp decrease (ΔEp =75mV) was found (Fig. 4A, curve d). These results demonstrated the successful preparation of the GQDs-CS/MB nanocomposite and the good electrochemical performance of the modified electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic activity of GQDs-CS/MB/GC electrode for reduction of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4B shows cyclic voltammograms of GCE (a), GQDs/GCE (b), GQDs-CS/GCE (c) and GQDs-CS/MB/GCE (d, e) in the absence (a,b,c,d) and presence (e) of 100μmolL−1 H2O2 in 0.1molL−1 sodium phosphate buffer containing 0.1molL−1 Na2SO4 solution (pH 7.4) at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. As can be seen, no redox peaks were apparent at bare GC (curve a), GQDs/GC (curve b) and GQDs-CS/GC (curve c) electrodes. However, a pair of symmetrical and well-defined redox peaks with a current ratio (Ipc/Ipa) of approximately 1 appeared at the GQDs-CS/MB/GCE. This confirmed that MB underwent reversible electrochemical reaction with the apparent formal peak potential (E0ʹ) of −344mV and peak-to-peak separation (ΔEp) of 103mV (curve d). As expected, a significant increase in the cathodic peak current was observed at −412mV in the presence of H2O2, whereas the anodic peak current decreased, thus indicating that the H2O2 in solution diffuses to the electrode surface leading to oxidize GQDs-CS/MB/GCEred into GQDs-CS/MB/GCEox. These results suggested that the immobilization of MB improves the efficiency of the electrocatalytic reduction of H2O2 and that the GQDs-CS is a suitable matrix for the electrodeposition of MB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and the type of supporting electrolyte
ce:section.ce:para:
The influence of pH, over the 1-12 range, on the cyclic voltammetric behavior of the GQDs-CS/MB/GC electrode was tested using 0.04molL−1 Britton-Robinson buffer solutions. Fig. 4C shows as both anodic and cathodic peak potentials of the single redox couple shifted towards less positive values as the pH increased. The inset on the top in Fig. 4C shows the dependence of the formal potential of MB electrodeposited on the GC/GQDs-CS electrode as a function of pH. The corresponding plot exhibits two linear sections at pH values below and above 6.0. The slopes values of these linear parts are −69.9mV/pH and −33.8mV/pH for pH < 6.0 and pH > 6.0, respectively. These slope values suggested that the redox reaction at pH < 6.0 is a two-electron two-proton-transfer reaction [21] while at pH > 6.0 is a two-electron one-proton transfer process. The intersection point of the two straight lines shown in inset a of Fig. 4C, pH=6.0, should correspond with the pKa value of the dimethylamino nitrogen in leuco methylene blue (LMB) [22,23]. The proposed reaction mechanism is shown in Scheme 1
[24].

Although the inset b of Fig. 4C shows that the largest anodic peak current was observed at pH 2.0, a phosphate buffer solution containing Na2SO4 (0.1M) of pH 7.4 was selected as the supporting electrolyte for subsequent experiments due to the better stability of H2O2 in the region of neutral pH and in view of future developments of this platform for biosensing applications [25,26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the potential scan rate
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of the potential scan rate on the voltammetric responses obtained at GQDs-CS/MB/GCE was checked in phosphate buffer solution containing Na2SO4 (0.1M) of pH 7.4. Fig. 5 shows as both the anodic and cathodic peak currents increased with increasing scan rates over the 10-900mV/s range. Furthermore, the redox potentials almost kept unchanged (peak-to-peak separation is about 20mV) at scan rates below 100 mVs−1. This behavior indicates that, as expected, MB electrodeposited on the electrode surface undergoes a reversible electron transfer. Inset A in Fig. 5 shows linear relationships between the peak currents and the scan rate in the whole range studied (10–900mVs−1), indicating a surface-controlled process. The regression equations, Ipa (μA)=0.1049 ν (mV s−1) – 1.2922 and Ipc (μA)=−0.0518 ν (mVs−1) – 3.9078, exhibited correlation coefficients of 0.9977 and 0.9925, respectively.

According to Laviron’s theory [27], the linear relationship between the peak current i p and the scan rate v can be described as:
(1)
i p
=
n 2
F 2
A Γ ν
4 R T
=
n F Q ν
4 R T
→ Γ =
Q
n F A
where Q is the quantity of charge involved in the reaction, F is Faraday constant, v is the scan rate, A and n stand for the real surface area of the modified electrode and the number of electron transfer, respectively. The symbols R, T and ip have their usual meanings. For the surface-controlled process, Q can be obtained by integrating the CV peaks of MB after the background correction. From the slope of the Ipa–v curve and equation (1), the average surface concentration of electroactive MB (Γ) at the GQDs-CS/MB/GCE was estimated to be 9.04×10−7 molcm−2, suggesting the effective immobilization of MB on GQDs-CS. The large surface coverage can be attributed to the expanded interspace and large surface area of GQDs-CS able to hold more MB molecules.

Furthermore, at relatively high scan rates, the peaks separation gradually increases with increasing scan rate, indicating a charge transfer kinetics limitation (Inset B in Fig. 5). By substitution of the slope values of the straight lines obtained from the Ep vs. log v plots in Laviron equation [28]:
(2)
l o g
k a
k c
= l o g
α
(
1 − α
)
where ka and kc are the slopes of the straight lines for Epa and Epc vs. log ν, respectively, a α value was calculated to be 0.5 and, therefore according to Laviron’s theory, the electron transfer number in the rate-determining step was found to be 1. Moreover, Tafel plot (log (I) versus E) at the scan rate of 10mVs−1 (InsetC in Fig. 5) provided a slope value ((1−α) n/0.059) of 9.73V decade−1 thus indicating (1-α) n value of 0.57 for 1 electron transfer process in the rate-determining step.

When ΔEp <200mV, the electron transfer rate constant ks could be estimated according to the ks =mnFν/RT equation [29], where n is the number of transferred electrons, ν the scan rate and m is a parameter related to the peak-to-peak separation. Hence, the average ks value was calculated to be 2.87s−1, which indicated a fast electron transfer process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chronoamperometric responses and estimation of the diffusion coefficient of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 6 shows the chronoamperometric responses at GQDs-CS/MB/GCE from solutions prepared in the absence (curve a) and in the presence (curves b–i: 0.10–0.87mM) of H2O2 in 0.1M phosphate buffer containing 0.1M Na2SO4 (pH 7.4), by applying a potential of −0.6V vs. SCE. As it is shown in inset A (Fig. 6), linear dependences were obtained by plotting net current values versus t−1/2 thus indicating that the electrocatalytic reduction of H2O2 was a diffusion-controlled process. Therefore, the diffusion coefficient (D) of H2O2 could be calculated according with Cottrell equation:
(3)
I = n F A
D
1 / 2
C
π
− 1 / 2
t
− 1 / 2
Where I is the current, A is the geometric surface area, n the number of electron transferred (n=2 for H2O2), C is the bulk concentration of H2O2, D is the diffusion coefficient (cm2s−1), and t is the elapsed time. From the slope value of the plot shown in inset B of Fig. 6, the diffusion coefficient of H2O2 was estimated to be 1.92×10−6 cm2 s−1, which is close to values reported previously of 1.76×10−6 [30] and 2.34×10−6 cm2 s−1 [31]. Chronoamperometry was also employed to evaluate the kcat according to the equation [32]:
(4)
I
c a t
I d
=
π
1 / 2
(
k
c a t
C t
)
1 / 2
where Id and Icat are the diffusion-controlled and the catalytic currents, respectively. From the slope value of the Icat/Id versus t1/2 plot (insetC in Fig. 6), the mean value of kcat for a given concentration of H2O2 (785μM) was found to be 4.45×104 cm3 mol−1 s−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of H2O2 at the GQDs-CS/MB/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic activity of the immobilized MB towards H2O2 reduction was evaluated by cyclic voltammetry, with the results shown in Fig. 7 A. As expected, with the successive addition of H2O2, the cathodic current measured at the GQDs-CS/MB/GCE was gradually increased, while the corresponding anodic current decreased indicating an electrocatalytic reduction process of H2O2. Since this behavior was not observed at the GQDs and GQDs-CS-modified GCEs, it was concluded that the electrocatalytic reduction of H2O2 can be attributed to the presence of electrodeposited MB. Cyclic voltammograms of 100μM H2O2 in 0.1M phosphate buffer solution containing 0.1M Na2SO4 (pH=7.4) recorded at different scan rates (results not shown) exhibited shifts of the peak potential for the electrocatalytic reduction of H2O2 to more negative values with increasing the sweep rate, suggesting a kinetic limitation in the electron transfer between the redox sites of MB and H2O2. However, the linear dependence of the reduction peak current of H2O2 with the square root of the scan rate confirmed that at sufficient overpotential the reaction is mass transport controlled [7], which is favorable for quantitative analysis [32]. In addition, the plot of the scan rate-normalized current (Ip/v 1/2) vs. ν showed a characteristic shape of an ErC′i electrocatalytic process [33].
According with this type of mechanism, the electrocatalytic reduction of H2O2 at the modified electrode surface could be explained as follows:

The overall reaction of (1) – (2) would be:
(3) H2O2 +2H+ +2e− →2H2O
The symbols Er and Cʹi denote reversible electrochemical and irreversible electrocatalytic chemical processes.

According with Andrieux and Saveant’s model for an ErCʹi mechanism [34], the relationship between the peak current and the concentration of substrate at low scan rates and large catalytic rate constant is given by the equation (5):
(5)
I p
= 0.496 n F A
D
1 / 2
(
F ν
R T
)
1 / 2
C s
where D and Cs are the diffusion coefficient (cm2 s−1 obtained by chronoamperometry) and the bulk concentration (molcm−3) of substrate (H2O2), respectively. Other symbols (n, A, F, R, T and ν) have their usual meanings. According to this theory, and using Fig. 1 in Ref. [34], the average value of kcat was calculated to be 4.3×104 cm3mol−1s−1 in good agreement with the value obtained in Section 3.7.

Steady-state amperometric responses at GQDs-CS/MB/GCE were obtained by performing successive additions of H2O2 at the constant applied potential of −0.6V vs SCE in PBS 0.1M, pH 7.4 containing 0.1M Na2SO4. The typical current-time curve displays rapid and sensitive response of the sensor which achieved 97% of the maximum current just 3s after H2O2 addition. The corresponding calibration plot exhibits good linear relationships between the cathodic amperometric current and H2O2 concentration in the ranges from 1 to 2900μM and 2.9–11.8mM (Fig. 7B). The occurrence of two linear ranges with different sensitivity may be attributed to this fact that while at low concentrations of H2O2, the electrocatalytic mechanism is dominant, the direct reduction of H2O2 on the electrode surface can play an important role in the measured analytical signal at larger concentrations [35]. A detection limit (LOD) of 0.7μM was calculated from on the S/N=3 criterion. Apart from providing one of the best LOD values, data summarized in Table 1 show as the sensor developed in this work offered the best sensitivity when compared with other electrochemical (bio)sensors reported for H2O2 determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility of the measurements and stability of GQDs-CS/MB/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Reproducibility and stability studies with the GQDs-CS/MB/GCE were performed by cyclic voltammetry in PBS 0.1M containing Na2SO4 0.1M (pH 7.4). The relative standard deviation (RSD) value calculated for the peak currents measured from five successive voltammograms in solutions containing 50μM H2O2 was 1.6%, thus demonstrating the good repeatability of measurements with the GQDs-CS/MB/GCEs. The fabrication reproducibility was tested by preparing five different GQDs-CS/MB/GCEs in the same way. The RSD value was 3.8% (50μM H2O2) indicating that the fabrication procedure of the GQDs-CS/MB/GCE was reliable and allowed reproducible electrochemical responses to be obtained with different sensors constructed in the same manner. The storage stability of GQDs-CS/MB/GCE was also evaluated by measuring the cathodic peak current recorded by cyclic voltammetry for 50μM H2O2 after storing the sensor in air at 25±1°C. The GQDs-CS/MB/GCE kept 97.8 and 90.2% of the initial response after 5days and 3 weeks of storage, respectively. These results indicated that GQDs-CS/MB/GCE provided good reproducibility and storage stability, which may be attributed to the high thermal stability and the strong adsorption of the MB by interaction with CS, which ensures minimal leaking out of MB from the modified electrode surface and therefore the maintenance of the electrocatalytic activity of GQDs-CS/MB hybrid material. These results also indicated that the electrochemical reaction of H2O2 at the modified electrode surface was done without generating a poisoning effect and that the GQDs-CS/MB/GCE was found to function over the course of several weeks without the need to renew the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference studies
ce:section.ce:para: The usefulness of a given sensor depends largely on its selectivity because some coexisting electroactive compounds in real samples may affect its performance. The influence of various compounds as potential interfering substances on the H2O2 determination was investigated by using amperometry at −0.6V vs. SCE under optimized conditions. The results obtained from Fig. 8 demonstrated that the addition of 1mM concentration of ascorbic acid, uric acid, dopamine, caffeine, glucose, and various inorganic salts (KNO3, Pb(NO3)2, Mg(NO3)2, AgNO3, Cd(NO3)2 NaNO2, NaF, and Na2SO4), had no significant effect on amperometric response for H2O2 0.5mM, obtaining in all cases deviations below 10%. These results demonstrated the great selectivity of the developed sensor, which made feasible its application to perform H2O2 determinations in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of the GQDs-CS/MB/GCEs was demonstrated by determining amperometrically H2O2 in spiked and non-spiked honey, pineapple juice, tap water and spring water samples using the standard addition method following the protocol described in section 2.5. As it is shown in Table 2 , the results provided by the developed sensor are in good agreement with the expected values providing recoveries between 98.0–100.6% (RSD=2.1%), 98.8–100.9% (RSD=2.7%) and 98.1–100.9% (RSD=2.3%) for analysis of honey, pineapple juice and water samples, respectively. Thus, GQDs-CS/MB/GCE can be successfully applied for reliable H2O2 determination in different food and water samples using straightforward protocols.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: This work describes for the first time the preparation and application of a novel nanocatalyst based on GQDs functionalized by CS and MB (GQDs-CS/MB) used to develop a non-enzymatic electrochemical sensor which allows the simple, low-cost, rapid, sensitive and selective determination of H2O2. Results demonstrated the stable electrodeposition of MB onto GQDs-CS and the efficient electrocatalytic activity of the resultant nanocomposite towards H2O2 reduction. For comparison purposes, other electrodes including bare GCE, GQDs/GCE and GQDs-CS/GCE were used for the control experiments. The synergistic effect between GQDs-CS and MB makes the resulting sensor to exhibit attractive electroanalytical characteristics such as simple operation procedure, low detection limit, wide linear range, fast response time, absence of interferences, good stability and reproducibility, and successful applicability for H2O2 determination in food and water samples with satisfactory recovery data. The excellent capabilities demonstrated by this new electrochemical scaffold also open new horizons in the development of non-enzymatic electrochemical sensors for the determination of other relevant analytes. It can be envisioned also that this new proposed sensor could be also a promising candidate to design and construct a rapid, low-cost and naked-eye portable kit for the detection of H2O2 by changing the colorless reduced form of MB (leucomethylene blue, LMB) to MB colored blue form in the present of H2O2 due to rapid oxidation of LMB by H2O2.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank the financial support from the University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran and of the CTQ2015-70023-R and CTQ2015-64402-C2-1-R (Spanish Ministerio de Economía y Competitividad Research Projects) and S2013/MT3029 (NANOAVANSENS Program from the Comunidad de Madrid). Authors would also like to thank Dr. Masih Darbandi from University of Tabriz for his valuable help.
